metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title to reflect all three delivered concerns, e.g., 'rebar-gerrit alerting hardening: confirm SNS subscription, add terraform drift CI, and codify orphan alarm', so the headline faithfully represents the full scope.
findings.finding: The plan title ('confirm/replace SNS subscription + add terraform drift CI') does not reflect AC3 (codifying and importing the orphan alarm) and still implies a 'replace' option that the plan body has already resolved as unnecessary. A reader relying on the title will underestimate scope and miss that a CloudWatch alarm import/apply is part of this ticket's post-merge operator steps.
findings.location: Plan title; AC3; Notes section
findings.suggested_fix: Retitle to: 'rebar-gerrit alerting hardening: confirm SNS subscription + add terraform drift CI + codify orphan disk alarm'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the CI-bootstrap gap in the plan: add a note that CI runs will fail until the post-merge apply creates the `rebar-terraform-plan` role, specify the expected duration/SLA for completing the apply, and document a rollback path (e.g. disable or skip the workflow) if the apply fails.
findings.finding: The drift-detection CI workflow (AC2b) will be merged to the repo before the `rebar-terraform-plan` OIDC IAM role (AC2a) exists in AWS, since the role is only created in the post-merge `terraform apply` operator step. Every CI run between merge and that apply will fail to authenticate, producing a broken-CI state of indeterminate duration. The plan does not acknowledge this transient failure window, provide a fallback authentication strategy, or specify a rollback path if the post-merge apply itself fails.
findings.location: AC2b; AC2a; Verification (post-merge operator step); Notes section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Notes: 'Between merge and the post-merge apply, terraform-drift CI runs will fail with an authentication error (role does not exist). The operator MUST complete the apply within [N hours] of merge. If the apply fails, the workflow should be temporarily disabled via the `on:` trigger or a `if: false` guard to prevent persistent CI noise, and the role creation retried before re-enabling.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2a: Add to the plan the explicit HCL mechanism for referencing the pre-existing OIDC provider ARN in the trust policy — either a `data "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider"` lookup block or the literal ARN string (`arn:aws:iam::896586841071:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com`) so the executor has an unambiguous, verifiable value to use as the `Federated` principal.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the `rebar-terraform-plan` IAM role's trust policy must reference the pre-existing GitHub OIDC provider (`token.actions.githubusercontent.com`) in account 896586841071, but does not specify the HCL mechanism to obtain that provider's ARN. A `Federated` trust-policy principal requires the exact ARN (`arn:aws:iam::896586841071:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com`). No `data "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider"` block is mentioned, and no hardcoded ARN is given — leaving the executor to invent the HCL wiring for this critical trust anchor.
findings.location: AC2a — OIDC role trust policy; infra/terraform/iam.tf (new role to be added)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to AC2a: 'Reference the existing provider via `data "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "github" { url = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" }` and set the trust policy Federated principal to `data.aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github.arn`.' This keeps the ARN resolved dynamically and avoids a hardcoded account-ID dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md` to the edit set (or explicitly note it needs no update), since it documents the manual terraform workflow that drift CI now augments — the plan currently names it as rationale but does not list it as a file to update.
findings.finding: infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md is a consumer of the infra/terraform workflow that explicitly documents the apply process as purely manual (steps 3, 'terraform plan … terraform apply') and makes no mention of CI. Adding drift-detection CI (AC2b) changes the operational model for this workflow, but the plan does not enumerate provision-restore.md as a file requiring update — leaving it as a misleading consumer that contradicts the new CI-enforced workflow.
findings.location: AC2b / Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief note to provision-restore.md's Step 3 block referencing the terraform-drift CI workflow and pointing to .github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml for automated drift detection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2a: Add a positive rationale for the `repo:navapbc/rebar:*` sub wildcard (e.g., 'needed so PRs touching infra/terraform trigger plan checks') OR tighten the sub condition to the specific workflow/ref and explain why, so the 'least-privilege' claim is consistent with the trust policy scope.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'least-privilege' for the rebar-terraform-plan OIDC role but specifies `repo:navapbc/rebar:*` as the sub condition without stating a positive rationale for the broad wildcard. This allows any branch, PR workflow, tag, or environment to assume the role; a tighter sub-claim (e.g., restricting to specific workflows or refs) would be more consistent with the stated least-privilege goal, and the plan gives no reason why the broad form is the right trade-off.
findings.location: AC2a — 'trust policy is scoped to repo:navapbc/rebar:* (sub condition)'
findings.suggested_fix: If PR-triggered plan checks are desired (the AC2b pull_request trigger implies they are), state explicitly: 'The `*` sub allows both PR branch workflows and push-to-main workflows to assume the role; scoping narrower would block PR plan checks. ReadOnlyAccess + SSM read is the blast-radius bound.' Alternatively, if PR checks are not needed, tighten to `repo:navapbc/rebar:ref:refs/heads/main` and drop the pull_request trigger.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `data "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "github_actions"` block (filtering on `url = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"`) to `infra/terraform/iam.tf` and reference its ARN in the `rebar-terraform-plan` role's trust policy, so the provider's existence is verified at every `terraform plan` rather than asserted out-of-band.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The GitHub OIDC provider (token.actions.githubusercontent.com) ALREADY EXISTS in account 896586841071 (verified)' as a prerequisite for AC2a, but no IaC in the repo manages or imports this provider, no data source guards against its absence, and no verification artifact (e.g., AWS CLI output, ticket comment) is cited. The trust policy on the new `rebar-terraform-plan` role references the provider as a string URL — if the provider is absent or was deleted, the CI auth silently fails at runtime rather than at plan/apply time.
findings.location: AC2a — 'The GitHub OIDC provider (token.actions.githubusercontent.com) ALREADY EXISTS in account 896586841071 (verified)'
findings.suggested_fix:
In `infra/terraform/iam.tf`, add:
```hcl
data "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "github_actions" {
  url = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
}
```
Then in the `rebar-terraform-plan` trust policy, reference `data.aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github_actions.arn` as the federated principal instead of a hardcoded string ARN. This causes `terraform plan` to fail fast (data source not found) if the provider is absent, rather than silently producing a broken role.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify using AWS-managed ReadOnlyAccess (instead of a custom policy scoped to the specific AWS services this workspace touches: EC2/CloudWatch/SNS/SSM/S3/KMS/IAM reads) — or replace it with a scoped custom policy to honour the 'least-privilege' claim.
findings.finding: AC2a claims 'least privilege' but specifies AWS-managed ReadOnlyAccess, which covers hundreds of actions across all AWS services — far broader than what terraform plan needs for this single-host workspace (EC2, CloudWatch, SNS, SSM, S3 state read, KMS, IAM read). The plan states 'least-privilege' as a design principle but then delegates to an unscoped managed policy without justification.
findings.location: AC2a — OIDC role (least-privilege, plan-only)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace ReadOnlyAccess with a custom aws_iam_policy in iam.tf enumerating only the read actions terraform plan needs: ec2:Describe*, cloudwatch:Describe*/List*/Get*, sns:Get*/List*, ssm:GetParameter*, s3:GetObject/ListBucket (on the state bucket only), kms:DescribeKey, iam:Get*/List*. This is strictly scoped and satisfies the 'least privilege' claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rationale in the plan (or as a workflow comment) for why `-lock=false` is acceptable for read-only drift-detection plan runs, acknowledging the race condition with concurrent operator applies and why it is tolerable (e.g. plan is idempotent to re-run, false-positive drift is harmless, applies are infrequent).
findings.finding: AC2b specifies `-lock=false` in the CI plan command, which silently bypasses the S3-native state locking that versions.tf (use_lockfile=true) establishes. The plan provides no rationale for bypassing the configured locking mechanism, and a concurrent operator apply + CI plan run could produce a confusing or inconsistent experience.
findings.location: AC2b — drift-detection CI; also versions.tf use_lockfile=true

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare whether the S3 state backend uses SSE-KMS with a key policy that denies the `rebar-terraform-plan` role's kms:Decrypt (i.e., the OIDC role can call s3:GetObject but gets an encrypted blob it cannot decrypt), OR narrow the OIDC role's S3 permissions to a specific bucket/prefix deny-list, OR acknowledge that state-stored values are acceptable to expose to the OIDC role and state that explicitly in AC2a.
findings.finding: The `rebar-terraform-plan` OIDC role receives ReadOnlyAccess, which includes s3:GetObject on all buckets. The Terraform S3 backend (rebar-tfstate-896586841071) stores state that includes values Terraform read with `with_decryption=true` from SSM SecureStrings (e.g., the alert-endpoint in monitoring.tf). If the bucket uses SSE-S3 (AES256) rather than SSE-KMS with a separate policy-controlled key, the OIDC role — assumable from any GitHub Actions run in the repo — can read the full state file and all sensitive values it contains. The plan declares 'plan-only, least privilege' but does not address this S3 state-read exposure or require a bucket KMS key that is separately denied to the OIDC role.
findings.location: AC2a — IAM role permissions; infra/terraform/versions.tf (backend S3 bucket); infra/terraform/monitoring.tf lines 31-34 (with_decryption=true data source)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2a, add an explicit disposition: either (a) confirm the state bucket uses SSE-KMS with a KMS key policy that excludes the rebar-terraform-plan role from kms:Decrypt (isolating state reads from secret decryption) — and add the appropriate kms_key_id to the backend block in versions.tf — or (b) replace the ReadOnlyAccess attachment with a custom scoped policy that grants only the specific actions terraform plan needs (e.g., cloudwatch:Describe*, sns:Get*, iam:Get*, ssm:GetParameter*, ec2:Describe*, s3:GetObject restricted to the state bucket key) and explicitly omits broad s3:GetObject on other paths.

findings.finding: AC2 (drift-detection CI) has no stated test that the CI pipeline actually fails when genuine drift exists; the AC is satisfiable by a pipeline that only runs 'terraform plan' on already-applied state, meaning the failure path (the whole point of the CI) is never exercised — a proxy-validation gap.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-time PR to infra/terraform/monitoring.tf with a dummy null_resource (never applied) and confirm the new CI workflow exits non-zero on that branch before merging the CI itself.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verification step that confirms the failure path: e.g., temporarily introduce a no-op HCL change (add a tag), run `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` locally, confirm exit code is 2, then revert — documenting the expected exit code in the Verification section.
findings.finding: The drift-detection CI's core failure path — a non-empty plan producing exit 2 causing the workflow to fail — is never exercised in the verification steps. The Verification section only checks the green path ('terraform-drift CI then reports green (no drift) on the next run'), leaving the actual drift-catches-drift behavior unproven.
findings.location: Verification section / AC2b
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Verification section: 'To confirm the failure path, introduce a scratch HCL change (e.g., add `tags = { drift-test = "1" }` to the role), run `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode`; confirm exit code is 2 (non-empty plan) and the CI step reports failure. Revert the scratch change before merge.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly whether `data.aws_instance.gerrit` already exists in infra/terraform, and if not, add a one-line instruction to AC3 specifying the lookup filter (e.g., `filter { name = "tag:Name" values = ["rebar-gerrit"] }`).
findings.finding: AC3 references `data.aws_instance.gerrit.id` as the InstanceId dimension source but does not confirm or require that this data source already exists in the HCL, nor does it instruct the executor to add it if missing. If it is absent, the HCL will fail `terraform validate`.
findings.location: AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'NOTE: `data "aws_instance" "gerrit" {}` with `filter { name = "tag:Name" values = ["rebar-gerrit"] }` already exists in infra/terraform/main.tf — no new data source needed.' (or instruct the executor to add it with the specified filter if it does not exist).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prose update to `infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md` (or a new ADR/CLAUDE.md section) documenting the new OIDC role, the drift-detection CI trigger logic, and when manual `terraform apply` is still required vs. CI-gated — so the runbook reflects the new workflow.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural pattern (GitHub Actions OIDC trust → AWS, terraform drift CI) but does not call out updating the existing infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md or adding an ADR/CLAUDE.md entry to capture this decision, leaving the runbook describing the old manual-apply-only workflow as the authoritative process doc.
findings.location: Notes / decisions section; AC2b; infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md (referenced but not updated)
findings.suggested_fix: Append a '## Terraform Drift CI' section to provision-restore.md explaining: (1) `.github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml` now runs plan on PR/push/daily; (2) the `rebar-terraform-plan` OIDC role is plan-only; (3) destructive applies still require operator creds and follow the existing manual steps below.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief decommission note (e.g., in Notes or a runbook reference) stating that `rebar-terraform-plan` IAM role and `terraform-drift.yml` must be removed if the rebar-gerrit host or repo is retired, so the shared-state lifecycle has a defined RETIRE path.
findings.finding: No RETIRE/cleanup path is defined for the `rebar-terraform-plan` IAM role or the `terraform-drift.yml` CI workflow if the gerrit host is decommissioned or the repo is archived. These artifacts will persist in the AWS account and GitHub Actions indefinitely, accumulating stale permissions.
findings.location: AC2a (infra/terraform/iam.tf), AC2b (.github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Notes section: 'RETIRE: if the rebar-gerrit host or rebar repo is decommissioned, run `terraform destroy -target=aws_iam_role.rebar_terraform_plan` and delete `.github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml` to avoid a dangling OIDC trust with ReadOnlyAccess.'

findings.finding: AC2 asserts 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071' as the authentication mechanism for the drift-detection CI, but no such OIDC provider or role exists anywhere in the repo's IaC (no aws_iam_openid_connect_provider, no token.actions.githubusercontent.com trust policy, no matching IAM role in any .tf file). The plan neither states it already exists nor specifies creating it, leaving the CI's auth prerequisite as an unbacked implicit assumption.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/ (GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-task or prerequisite to AC2: 'Create aws_iam_openid_connect_provider for token.actions.githubusercontent.com and an aws_iam_role with a repo-scoped (repo:navapbc/rebar:*) trust policy in infra/terraform/ (or infra/bootstrap/) before wiring the CI workflow.'

findings.finding: AC2 asserts 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071' as though it already exists, but no aws_iam_openid_connect_provider resource and no GitHub Actions IAM role appear anywhere in infra/terraform/. This is an unverified capability assertion at a security/auth integration bright line: the OIDC provider for token.actions.githubusercontent.com must be registered in the account before any CI workflow can assume a role via it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource for `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com` and an `aws_iam_role` with a condition-scoped assume-role policy (e.g. `repo:navapbc/rebar:*`) to `infra/terraform/iam.tf` or a new `infra/terraform/iam_cicd.tf`. Cite these resources explicitly in AC2 so the plan is self-contained.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071' as a ready-to-use auth precondition for the drift-detection CI workflow, but no OIDC provider or OIDC-capable IAM role exists anywhere in infra/terraform/ (zero matches for 'aws_iam_openid_connect_provider', 'token.actions.githubusercontent', 'oidc'). The plan does not specify whether this is a new HCL resource to add, a manually pre-created role, what IAM permissions it requires (state-bucket read, lockfile write, CloudWatch/EC2/SNS read for plan), or how the OIDC thumbprint and audience are configured. Without this role the CI workflow cannot authenticate to AWS at all.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/ (GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit prerequisite to AC2: 'Add `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` for `token.actions.githubusercontent.com` and an `aws_iam_role` (e.g. `rebar-github-actions-terraform-plan`) with a scoped read-only policy (S3 state read, all resources `terraform plan` reads) to `infra/terraform/iam.tf`. The CI workflow assumes this role via `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4` with `role-to-assume`. Document the role ARN as a repo variable.'

findings.finding: `terraform plan` exits with code 0 even when there are pending changes to apply — the CI job as described would PASS on drift, not fail, unless `-detailed-exitcode` (exit 2 on diff) is specified. The AC says 'fails on un-applied drift' but the mechanism as written does not achieve that behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan... and fails on un-applied drift'
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC2 to read: '...runs `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` on changes under infra/terraform/ and fails (exit 2) when there are un-applied changes.'

findings.finding: `terraform plan` exits 0 both when there are NO changes and when there ARE planned changes (drift); it only exits non-zero (1) on hard errors. The AC states the CI 'fails on un-applied drift', but a naive `terraform plan` step in CI will always pass when terraform itself succeeds — even when drift is detected. The correct invocation requires `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode`, which exits 2 when changes are present, allowing CI to fail on drift.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'fails on un-applied drift'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC2 wording to: 'runs `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` … and fails (exit 2) on un-applied drift'. In the workflow step: `run: terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -out=tfplan` with `continue-on-error: false`.

findings.finding: AC2 introduces a GitHub OIDC → AWS IAM trust relationship (an external lower-trust actor gaining AWS credentials) but does not declare the OIDC sub-claim conditions that restrict which GitHub repo/branch/workflow/environment can assume the role, nor the IAM permissions (actions + resources) the role will hold.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'A drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on changes under infra/terraform/ (GitHub OIDC role in account 896586841071)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the new IaC for the CI role, add a trust-policy condition such as: `StringEquals: {"token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub": "repo:navapbc/rebar:ref:refs/heads/main"}` (or a `StringLike` wildcard covering only PR branches in that repo). Grant only the minimum AWS permissions `terraform plan` requires: `s3:GetObject`, `s3:ListBucket` on `arn:aws:s3:::rebar-tfstate-896586841071`, plus a tightly scoped `iam:Get*`, `ec2:Describe*`, `cloudwatch:Describe*`, `sns:Get*`, `ssm:DescribeParameters` set. State the chosen permission boundary explicitly in the plan's AC2 text.

findings.finding: AC3 uses the vague verb 'reconcile' without specifying the intended disposition of the orphan alarm (import into Terraform state, codify in HCL, or delete), deferring a real design decision to the executor.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3

findings.finding: AC3 ('Reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high') leaves the ownership and retirement path undefined — 'reconcile' could mean import-into-state, add HCL, or delete — so neither a human nor an automated agent can determine when the AC is satisfied or how to avoid leaking the live resource.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3

findings.finding: The drift-detection CI (AC2) is scoped to 'changes under infra/terraform/' (i.e., PR-triggered), which does not catch the scenario it was designed to prevent: HCL already committed to main but not applied. A scheduled or post-merge plan run is needed to surface existing drift.
findings.location: AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second GitHub Actions workflow triggered by `schedule: cron` (e.g., daily) that runs `terraform plan` against the main branch state and fails/notifies on any diff, in addition to the PR-time check.

findings.finding: AC3 ('reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high') specifies no chosen mechanism — import into terraform state (keeping the alarm) vs. delete from AWS (removing alerting coverage). These have opposite operational outcomes and the correct choice depends on whether the alarm is intentional coverage, yet the plan leaves the choice entirely open.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Reconcile the incidental orphan alarm rebar-gerrit-data-disk-high (live but absent from HCL/state)'

findings.finding: No concrete end-to-end validation mechanism is specified for either the SNS subscription fix or the drift-detection CI; the ACs state desired system state but not how a human or automated check would confirm the alerting chain actually works for a stakeholder.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 and AC2
findings.suggested_fix: AC1 addendum: 'Validated by: manually publishing a test SNS message and confirming receipt at the subscriber.' AC2 addendum: 'Validated by: a CI run on a branch with a dummy resource added to monitoring.tf that has never been applied — workflow must exit non-zero.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
